By default `ProcessEndpoint` [captures stderr from the subprocess and relays it to the Twisted logging stream](https://github.com/twisted/twisted/blob/twisted-17.5.0/src/twisted/internet/endpoints.py#L355-L368). I think that it would be reasonable to pass the error log path as an argument and have the child open it. Any output during startup will still be captured by Twisted and sent to Trial's log file, and thereafter the child process can manage its own output.

What output is there after startup anyway? The child process doesn't initialize logging (maybe it should...), so probably just Python warnings and any terminal traceback.

`from openfisca_core import periods`

This is confusing and I'm rewriting it, but to answer the question:
- `value_json[str(period)] = values_dict = {}` creates a `dict`, stores it in a variable named `values_dict`, and also assign `value_json[str(period)]` to this `dict`.
- Assigning values to `values_dict` is equivalent to assigning values to `value_json[str(period)]`, as these two reference the same dictionary.
- In the end, the returned value `value_json ` has been modified by the assignation

The goal of `values_dict ` is just to be a shortcut towards ` value_json[str(period)]`.

It is not safe to reset all these under all conditions. It depends on where you got the resume token. Essentially you need to clear out everything you got after the resume token. But some of this might have come before the resume token. For eg:
Row just has a single string column and these are the partial result sets you get

PRS1: value: "h", chunked: true, resume_token: "zjgfdkjf"
PRS2: value: "e", chunked: true, resume_token: Null

Say when you try to read next PRS3 the stream breaks. So you resume using the token yielded by PRS1. The resumed stream will yield PRS2 onwards. But since you cleared pending_chunk, you lost PRS1.

This flag shouldn't be set unless we're integrating with a handwritten library. So if we're scaling to an unbounded amount of APIs, this flag will essentially never be set because handwritten libraries don't scale to an unbounded amount.

It seems to me that keeping a machine readable list of APIs that require this flag is doing the exact same thing as setting this flag (i.e., must set one line of code per API per language with source delivery that cares about package metadata), except that it introduces yet another input in yet another location to the generation logic.

If there's a solution that lets us integrate with handwritten libraries without having to set a configuration flag, I'm all for it.
